However, there are some key differences between these two classes of chemicals in terms of their toxicokinetics and toxicodynamics.

(A) Unlike inhibition by organophosphates, carbamate-induced inhibition of cholinesterase is typically reversible and shorter-lasting. This is because carbamates form a carbamylated complex with the enzyme that spontaneously hydrolyzes, allowing the enzyme to regenerate. In contrast, organophosphates form a more stable phosphorylated complex with the enzyme, which can only be regenerated through a process called aging, which takes a longer time.

(B) Carbamates are generally more rapidly biotransformed in mammalian systems compared to organophosphates. This is because carbamates are more susceptible to hydrolysis by esterases and other enzymes, which can quickly break down the carbamate molecule into less toxic metabolites. In contrast, organophosphates are more resistant to enzymatic degradation and can persist in the body for a longer time.

(C) The depression of plasma acetylcholinesterase is not a good indicator of the severity of poisoning by carbamates. This is because carbamates have a shorter duration of action and are more rapidly reversible than organophosphates. As a result, the inhibition of plasma cholinesterase may not accurately reflect the extent of carbamate exposure or the severity of toxicity.

(D) The mechanism by which carbamates and organophosphates elicit their toxicity is not identical. While both classes of chemicals can inhibit cholinesterase, they do so through different mechanisms. Carbamates form a carbamylated complex with the enzyme, while organophosphates form a phosphorylated complex. Additionally, carbamates are typically more reversible and shorter-lasting than organophosphates, as mentioned earlier.

(E) Unlike organophosphate poisoning, carbamate poisoning does not typically result in delayed neuropathy. This is because carbamates do not inhibit neuropathy target esterase (NTE), an enzyme that is involved in the development of delayed neuropathy. In contrast, some organophosphates can inhibit NTE, leading to delayed neuropathy in severe cases.
